His name graced the sides of Indy cars and stock cars. Top Fuel dragsters streaked across the finish line, his engineering thumping away inside their supercharged Hemi engines. On the Bonneville Salt Flats, Isky was the unseen hand propelling such legends of speed as Mickey Thompson to record-breaking velocities. Ed Isky Iskenderian earned his nickname of The Camfather through performance-enhancing camshaft designs and brilliant marketing campaigns that brought the thrill and skill of a Richard Petty Daytona lap or a Don Garlits quarter-mile to every kid building a hotted-up car in their own garage. His influence can be felt in every form of motorsport today, and Isky lived long enough to enjoy the changing landscape of the industry he helped pioneer. Today we say farewell to Ed Iskenderian, who died on Feb 4, 2026 at the impressive age of 104 years old. Iskenderian was born July 10, 1921, in Tulare County, California. He was the son of immigrants from Armenia and retained a pride in his heritage throughout his life, asking anyone with dark hair and dark eyes if they might also be Armenian, and then, if they were not, kindly reassuring them with a joking, Well, that's okay too. For Sale Near You See all results for used cars for sale near 30038 California was a hotbed of hot rodding, and Isky quickly fell in love with the modified Model Ts he saw the older kids in the neighborhood tinkering with. He built his first car while still in his teens, and he would later race that roadster on the dry lake beds surrounding Los Angeles, achieving speeds of more than 120 mph. Isky was a patriot and served in the U.S. Air Force during World War II. He often joked that he wanted to be a pilot but wasn't handsome enough, a perfect example of the self-deprecating but always good-natured humor that marked his attitude toward the world. When he returned to California after the war, Isky bought a cam-grinding machine and began working on reprofiling camshafts in the pursuit of more power, higher revs, and better fuel management. His cams began attracting attention from stock car racers and drag racers, and Isky was quick to see the marketing possibilities in sponsorship and merchandising with names including Richard Petty, Don Garlits, Don Prudhomme, and Jim Clark. He'd go on to provide cams for eminent racers in every form of American motorsport, from boats to land speed. ed iskenderian with camshafts / Elana Scherr / Car and Driver His advertisements in Hot Rod and Car Craft were funny and provocative, poking fun at competitors and inventing speedy-sounding phrases like the five-cycle cam. Isky also gets credit among many in the automotive industry as the inventor of the graphic T-shirt, offering racers and friends white Ts printed with the Isky logo long before such apparel became the established uniform of cool teens across the globe. Iskenderian not only dressed the automotive industry, he also helped legitimize and expand it, as one of the founding members of the Speed Equipment Manufacturers Association (now SEMA, and standing for Specialty Equipment Market Association). Even if you've never shopped for lifters and pushrods for your own build, someone in your circle has had the thrill of a bigger dyno number or a sweet choppy idle after switching to an Isky cam. Even the performance arms of today's OEM manufacturers owe a hat tip to Isky for his enthusiastic pursuit of better-running engines and new speed records. Ed Iskenderian is survived by his children, Richard, Timothy, and Amy; eight grandchildren; his brother Ben; his company, Isky Racing Cams; and an extended family of sponsored racers, builders, and friends. Headshot of Elana Scherr Senior Editor, Features Like a sleeper agent activated late in the game, Elana Scherr didn't know her calling at a young age. Like many girls, she planned to be a vet-astronaut-artist, and came closest to that last one by attending UCLA art school. She painted images of cars, but did not own one. Elana reluctantly got a driver's license at age 21 and discovered that she not only loved cars and wanted to drive them, but that other people loved cars and wanted to read about them, which meant somebody had to write about them. Since receiving activation codes, Elana has written for numerous car magazines and websites, covering classics, car culture, technology, motorsports, and new-car reviews. In 2020, she received a Best Feature award from the Motor Press Guild for the C/D story A Drive through Classic Americana in a Polestar 2. In 2023, her Car and Driver feature story In Washington, D.C.'s Secret Carpool Cabal, It's a Daily Slug Fest was awarded 1st place in the 16th Annual National Arts & Entertainment Journalism Awards by the Los Angeles Press Club.

Ford and Geely are in talks to potentially partner on manufacturing and shared technology, according to a new report from Reuters. Eight people familiar with the situation reportedly told Reuters that the partnership may see Geely utilize a European Ford factory to build cars for the region. The Reuters report is the second story this week tying Ford to a Chinese automaker, after a story from the Financial Times linked the American company to Xiaomi. For the second time in a week, Ford is being pegged as a potential partner to a Chinese automaker, according to a new report from Reuters. Citing eight people with knowledge of the matter, Reuters said that Ford and Geely are in discussions for a potential partnership. 2025 guangzhou international automobile exhibition / 2025 Guangzhou International Automobile Exhibition / Anadolu / Getty Images / Geely Galaxy According to the report, the discussions are ongoing and wide-ranging. Three people familiar with the meetings said that Geely may use Ford factory space in Europe to produce vehicles for the region. According to two of Reuters' sources, the two automakers are also considering a framework for shared vehicle technologies, including some related to automated driving, though the report says that the talks focused on European manufacturing are further along. Ford Vehicles For Sale Near You See all results for used Ford for sale near 50317 Ford reportedly sent a delegation to China this week, which followed meetings that took place between Geely and Ford leadership in Michigan the week before. Several Geely-owned brands are sold in the United States (including legacy brands like Lotus and Volvo), though high tariff rates and a ban on cars with Chinese software have effectively stopped the U.S. sale of cars built in China. The Breakdown Audi's cheapest EV arrives later this year. It's expected to dust off the A2 name. With the A1 and Q2 retiring, the A2 will become the entry-level model. Audi is gearing up for a busy 2026, with multiple product launches locked in for the remainder of the year. While enthusiasts are eagerly awaiting the new RS5, the SUV-loving crowd has its eyes on the next-generation Q7 and the first-ever Q9. At the other end of the lineup, Ingolstadt is set to shake things up by both adding and subtracting models. Long confirmed to bow out after the current generation, the A1 supermini is expected to retire by year's end. The subcompact hatchback will likely exit the lineup alongside its crossover sibling, the Q2. Indirectly replacing both models will be a completely different newcomer, reviving a moniker last used about two decades ago: A2. While the original A2 was far from a commercial success due to its high price and quirky styling, Audi hopes its spiritual successor will fare better. The German luxury brand has already confirmed plans to introduce an entry-level EV in 2026, and senior sources within the company have told Autocar it will carry the A2 designation. Photo by: Audi Our exclusive rendering draws on recent spy shots of a camouflaged prototype undergoing final testing. It won't be as polarizing as the original A2, nor will it fully embrace the new design language previewed by the Concept C. The small electric hatchback was penned while Marc Lichte was still overseeing Audi's design team, before Massimo Frascella was appointed Head of Design in 2024. It remains to be seen whether Frascella has left his mark on the A2, but logic suggests the car's design had already been frozen by the time he arrived in Ingolstadt. Whatever the case, Audi's cheapest electric vehicle will feature split headlights. Elsewhere, traditional door handles are absent, replaced by tiny winglets integrated into the beltline for a layout also seen on the Ford Mustang Mach-E. The newcomer will predictably slot below the Q4 E-Tron and is likely to carry the same suffix as Audi's other EVs. The A2 E-Tron will be closely related to the Volkswagen ID.3, possibly the updated version due soon. Interestingly, Autocar notes that Audi aims to lure buyers who miss the BMW i3 hatchback, which was retired in 2022. Coincidentally, Munich is also reviving the i3 name, though it's being repurposed for an electric sedan in the 3 Series segment. Given Audi's premium positioning, the A2 is expected to command a price premium over its mainstream ID.3 sibling. VW charges €33,330 for the base version in Germany, so it's reasonable to assume the tuxedo-wearing cousin will cost north of €35,000. Photos - Audi A2, historical photos Motor1's Take: The new A2 will be an intriguing addition to an electric luxury segment dominated by far larger and more expensive vehicles. Its styling likely won't be to everyone's taste, but we appreciate Audi for injecting some of the original model's funkiness into its indirect successor. We wouldn't count on seeing the A2 E-Tron in the United States, given its small footprint. After all, VW doesn't sell the ID.3 in North America either. In Europe, however, it stands a better chance of success than the original A2, helped by the steadily growing appetite for EVs. In 2025, purely electric cars accounted for 19.5 percent of all vehicles sold across the 27 EU countries, plus the UK, Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland. Source: Autocar We want your opinion! What would you like to see on Motor1.com? Take our 3 minute survey. - The Motor1.com Team

The Breakdown VW's workhorse gets a GTI aura with a beefy body kit. The Transporter Sportline has a lowered suspension and 19-inch wheels. It's all show with no extra go as the engines are left untouched. Ford may have a long history of selling sporty-looking vans in Europe, but Volkswagen Commercial Vehicles now wants a piece of that action as well. To make it happen, it's giving the Transporter a new range-topping trim that channels a GTI vibe into a genuinely attractive hauler. With no real business case for a performance-oriented van, however, the Sportline is all show, with no extra go. Since we mentioned Ford, it's worth remembering that the latest Transporter is largely based on the Transit Custom. In this new GTI-esque configuration, it receives a beefy body kit available in several flavors. While the images show the five-seat Kombi version, customers can also opt for the panel van. Both body styles are offered in short- and long-wheelbase configurations. Beyond its meaner styling, this spicy Transporter stands out with exclusive 19-inch wheels that ditch the usual VW logos in favor of Sportline branding. It also gets a lowered sports suspension with Eibach springs, dropping the ride height by 1.1 inches (29 millimeters). Photo by: Volkswagen The most daring feature is found at the rear, where a split roof spoiler makes it clear this isn't your typical workhorse. Up front, the grille's red accent stripe looks lifted straight from the Polo GTI or Golf GTI. If you'd rather have a true GTI model that hasn't been sold before, the ID.2 Polo GTI is coming later this year, albeit without a combustion engine. As the flagship version, the Sportline comes loaded as standard. VW includes everything from matrix LED headlights and roof rails to a heated steering wheel and illuminated metal door sill plates. The GTI-like red finish is contrasted by glossy black accents, including the electrically adjustable, heated mirrors. Mind you, this isn't the first time the Transporter has received the Sportline treatment. VW gave the previous-generation T6.1 a more aggressive makeover a few years ago. As with its predecessor, the newcomer leaves the drivetrains untouched. Whether buyers choose diesel, plug-in hybrid, or fully electric power, there's no extra grunt over a regular model. 2026 Volkswagen Transporter Sportline Motor1's Take: Vans aren't supposed to be sporty, but they certainly look more appealing when impersonating a GTI. The Transporter Sportline serves as an alternative to the mechanically related Ford Transit Custom vans modified by MS-RT and sold directly through Ford Pro. Europeans are spoiled for choice when it comes to vans that look fast but actually aren't. Stellantis, for example, is well versed in this niche, offering the Citroën Dispatch VTR, Opel Vivaro GS, Fiat Scudo Onyx, and Peugeot Expert Sport. Renault's Trafic Extra Sport is more subtle, but it, too, tries to inject some excitement into the typically dull van segment. Source: Volkswagen Commercial Vehicles We want your opinion! The Breakdown Nissan gave US dealers an early look at the revived Xterra. The styling is claimed to be a modernized version of the original. A luxury version badged as an Infiniti is also coming. Selling cars in today's fiercely competitive market isn't easy, but the job does come with perks. Automakers typically gain access to new products years ahead of launch, giving them plenty of time to prepare for the arrival of fresh metal. Such is the case for Nissan dealers, who have been allowed an early look at the revival of a long-awaited nameplate: the Xterra. This week in Las Vegas, Nissan showed the new Xterra behind closed doors during a North American Automotive Dealer Show (NADA) meeting. Automotive News cites Scott Smith, president of the Smith Automotive Group near Atlanta, saying the hotly anticipated SUV will be worth the wait. The two-row off-roader is described as radical-looking, with design cues inspired by the original model but modernized for the late 2020s. The resurrected Xterra is expected to ride on a newly developed body-on-frame platform, with a design that has an aggression to it, according to Charlie Hicks, CEO of Hicks Automotive Group in Texas. Those who have seen it say Nissan is aiming for a rugged look, featuring a beefy grille and an overall emphasis on toughness. Photo by: Nissan The third-generation Xterra is slated to launch in the second half of 2028 and should cost less than $40,000, according to the company's Senior Vice President, Michael Soutter. AN reports the brawny, go-anywhere vehicle won't arrive alone, as a more luxurious Infiniti counterpart is also planned, presumably with a higher price tag. Nissan has a busy agenda ahead, since the new architecture is also expected to underpin the next Pathfinder, Frontier, and QX60. All of these models are said to offer electrified powertrains, meaning hybrid and/or plug-in hybrid options. Meanwhile, the next-generation Rogue should break cover later this year and reach the market in 2027. While the NADA meeting mostly revolved around SUVs and trucks, one car was also mentioned by Nissan. Dealers learned the seven-year-old Altima is getting a stay of execution, but the midsize sedan will inevitably bow out at the end of summer 2027. There's no mention of a successor, leaving the new Sentra as the sole surviving car following the recent axing of the entry-level Versa. Motor1's Take: A new Xterra can't come soon enough, as rugged SUVs remain all the rage in North America. Reviving a once-popular nameplate and leaning into off-road capability with a two-speed transfer case (including low range) should appeal to buyers seeking alternatives to the Toyota 4Runner. The Infiniti version, however, may face an unexpected rival by the time it reaches the market. BMW is reportedly plotting its own rugged SUV to lure buyers away from the Mercedes G-Class and Land Rover Defender. Source: Automotive News We want your opinion! An Illinois woman posts a video of the damage to her Cadillac Escalade after a minor parking mishap. Viewers are urging her to get a vehicle compatible with her driving abilities. Are they being fair, or is this advice over the line? TikTok creator Staysee (@stayseebeee) posted a video in the aftermath of her single-car collision on Jan. 23. Well, my day's ruined, she says to start the video. You want to see what I just did to my Escalade? Staysee hops out of the Escalade and shows viewers a long scratch in the side, which was presumably caused by Staysee brushing up against a yellow parking pole or another traffic calming device. In the caption, Staysee writes, I'm just a girl… Viewers React To The State Of Illinois Woman's Escalade In the comments section, viewers expressed exasperation with both Staysee's use of the I'm just a girl meme and her driving ability. I'm sorry, but 'I'm just a girl' is so insulting to those of us who don't think this behavior is cute, said one viewer. That's Altima-level driving, said a second person. A third person argued, As an Escalade owner, I know it has sensors and cameras all over the place. Staysee replied, Welp, they didn't make a peep. Another person said, If you hit stuff regularly, that's probably an indicator you shouldn't be driving a car that big. Someone else argued, You need a Toyota Camry. Not a $130,000 Escalade. Is The Cadillac Escalade Too Big for Some Drivers? According to Cadillac of Norwood, drivers should consider their lifestyle and driving habits when deciding which Escalade to choose. The standard Escalade is easier to maneuver than the ESV, which has an extra seat and legroom. But that is a distinction without a difference for some consumers—some of whom have been complaining about the scale of the Escalade for a while now. Four years ago, a Reddit user panned the latest model on those grounds. Others in the comments said that's the point. The only reason you buy an Escalade is for it to be big, one said. And it does that great. Back in 2021, The Verge reviewed the Escalade and called it among the most stressful vehicles to drive due to its heft. TractionLife also noted the Escalade can feel very cumbersome in city driving and especially when parking. When it comes to passenger safety, the Escalade is actually considered among the brand's top models. That's due to both the parking assistance systems (which apparently didn't go off in Staysee's case) and its large frame. Motor1 contacted Staysee and General Motors via email for comment. A man takes his used Mercedes-Benz in for what he expects to be a routine oil change. Unfortunately, the mechanic finds several signs of past technician malpractice under the hood. TikTok creator Cxllmedez (@cxllmedez) is a Toronto mechanic who posted the video about the situation on Jan. 24. He starts the video by saying, If you're thinking about buying a 10+-year-old Mercedes-Benz, pay attention. Man Brings In 10-Year-Old Mercedes-Benz For Oil Change The TikToker explains, This vehicle right here came in for just an oil change. Upon putting it in the air, you can see… we just have a complete mess. The Mercedes is clearly leaking oil. As you can see, someone took it upon themselves to apply [what] looks like shower caulk on this oil pan, Cxllmedez says. Now, this oil pan has a plastic seal. No sealant needs to be applied. That's not the only issue Cxllmedez found with the Mercedes; the customer said he recently purchased it used. We had an aftermarket drain plug, which this does have a rubber seal on it, he says. It was the incorrect size, so we were leaking oil from there as well. Not only that, but he says there were also incorrect bolts and washers throughout the system, causing additional leaks. We also went ahead and resealed these upper cam covers. As you can see, they do look a lot cleaner now, he adds. To conclude the video, Cxllmedez offers a bit of advice for car owners. The main thing with this is: be careful where you take your vehicle to get worked on, he says. If you're not going to take it to the dealership, take it to somewhere that is trusted. Viewers React To The 10-Year-Old Mercedes With Major Oil Leak In the comments section, viewers reacted to the situation the Mercedes owner found himself in, the state of the brand, and the best way to get one of its models serviced. The problem isn't the car, said one viewer. The problem is people buying cars they can't afford so they ignore the regular maintenance and service. A plastic oil pan is crazy, said a second viewer. Mercedes has fallen off if you ask me. I have a 2003 Mercedes-Benz with over 300K miles now and still runs great, but best believe the maintenance is done every time, and I have paperwork to show for it, a third person said. Mercedes are great if you do the maintenance constantly. Another person argued, If the vehicle would have been serviced by a reputable mechanic, then it wouldn't have these issues. Is It Smart to Buy a 10-Year-Old Luxury Vehicle? It can be a good idea to buy a 10-year-old luxury vehicle, but it really depends on a combination of factors, including value, mileage, and maintenance and accident history. Car buyers really have to shop around, get used vehicles inspected by a third-party inspector, and use their own best judgment to get a good deal. Some drivers might be attracted to the look, feel, and drivability of older luxury vehicles. However, CarMax points out that those vehicles also come with serious drawbacks, including expiring warranty coverage, higher maintenance costs, and outdated electronics. Essentially, your mileage may vary. Motor1 contacted Cxllmedez via TikTok comment and direct message for comment. We also contacted Mercedes-Benz via press email for comment. We've got good news and bad news for fans of BMW convertibles. We'll start with the latter. BMW no longer makes a convertible variant of its two-door 2-series, which means Bimmer aficionados looking to nab a brand-new softtop from the German brand will have to make do with the 4-series convertible, a model that starts at $61,950 and measures more than half a foot longer than the current 2-series coupe. The good news is that there are plenty of previous-generation 2-series convertibles, meaning used 2015 BMW 2-series convertibles to used 2021 BMW 2-series convertibles, available with less than 100,000 miles for under $20,000. The options expand even further if you open up your search to four-cylinder 228i and 230i convertibles. We, however, are fiends for power. While we won't fault you for saving a few bucks on a four-cylinder 2-series 'vert, we really think you ought to consider a 2-series droptop in six-cylinder 10Best-winning M235i or M240i form. Preferably one with the six-speed manual gearbox. That said, beggars can't be choosers, and settling on an M235i or M240i convertible with the eight-speed automatic is no penalty box. In fact, the self-shifting gearbox arguably better fits the laid-back open-air vibe of the M235i and M240i convertibles. As the numerals in the names suggest, M240i convertibles, meaning used 2017 BMW M240i convertibles to used 2021 BMW M240i convertibles, offer more power from their forced induction 3.0-liter straight-six engine. Specifically, 335 horsepower. That's 15 more ponies than the boosted 3.0-liter in earlier M235i convertibles, meaning used 2015 BMW M235i convertibles and 2016 BMW M235i convertibles. Regardless, both the M235i convertible and M240i convertible include a pair of small back seats. Space is limited, so don't plan to use this BMW for carpool duty. Still, the ability to cart up to three friends (even if your friends in the back seats will surely regret their decision) is a bonus that many other fun-to-drive convertibles at this pricepoint do without.

From the January 1987 issue of Car and Driver. Unless you're a devout Road & Track reader and a big fan of cork gaskets and oil stains on your driveway, you probably never mourned the passing of the British car industry in the U.S. One by one such time-honored marques as MG, Triumph, Austin, and Rover (not to mention Hum­ber, Daimler, Hillman, Jensen, Sunbeam, Vauxhall, and Wolseley) slit their corpo­rate throats on the steely knives of the American marketplace. With the excep­tion of Lotus, Jaguar, Rolls-Royce, and, recently, Panther, all the British carmak­ers came to the same conclusion about the American market: If we couldn't accom­modate ourselves to their cars, then they didn't want our business. The introduction of the Sterling signals the return of a major British player to the U.S.: the Austin Rover Group. Please hold the jokes about SU carburetors and oil drip trays until you finish reading this. ARG has formed an alliance with no less a force than Honda. If Sterlings do drip oil, the leaks will be the finest that Japanese engineering can produce. Sterling Vehicles For Sale Near You See all results for used Sterling for sale near 33947 1987 Sterling 825SL / Aaron Kiley / Car and Driver The Sterling 825S and 825SL (base and full-boat deluxe) will be imported by Austin Rover Cars of North America (ARCONA). The Austin Rover Group of England, which is owned by the British government, owns 49 percent of ARCONA; the remaining 51 percent is owned by Braman Enterprises. Norman Braman, the chairman of ARCONA and the man who forged this enterprise, is your average overachieving, hyper­-successful car dealer (Audi, BMW, Cadil­lac, Honda, Porsche, Rolls-Royce, Toy­ota). For good measure, he also owns the Philadelphia Eagles. Like the organization that sells it, the Sterling is also a hybrid—mostly Japanese but with some English thrown in. Mechan­ically, the Sterling is nearly identical to the Acura Legend. The two cars are the prod­uct of a joint venture between ARG and Honda, and they were conceived and de­veloped simultaneously. ARG likes to stress the point that this was no mere sub­contract venture but a genuine collabora­tion. It began with the two companies star­ing at the same clean sheet of paper and trying to come up with a pair of automo­biles that would appeal to buyers in Japan, in Europe, and in the U.S. 1987 sterling 825sl / 1987 Sterling 825SL / Aaron Kiley / Car and Driver With the blindfold off, the job is a lot easier, especially in the SL edition of the Sterling. The full-house, maximum-effort model is dressed in Connolly leather and equipped with your choice of a manual or an automatic transmission at no extra cost, a trip computer, a heated driver's seat, an eight-speaker stereo, alloy wheels, and power seats with four memory presets (even the rear-view-mirror settings are memorized). A host of other goodies—­A/C, a power sunroof, power windows, burled-walnut door and dash trim, map lights, and full instrumentation—are stan­dard on both the S and the SL. You can have the ABS, leather, alloy wheels, and automatic on the S, too, but you'll pay for them a la carte. The only option on the SL is, get this, a power-reclining rear seat. It's a neat doodad, but it really eats up kneeroom. All this may sound like wretched excess, but it works. The Connolly-covered seats are built and bolstered for the long haul. We spent twelve straight hours in them and never once had to call for a skeletal recalibration. The three-spoke steering wheel is appropriately beefy for the prop­er sporting feel, all the major controls are less than a finger's length away, the instru­ment cluster provides all the necessary in­formation, and the trip computer is so easy to use that you don't need to peek at the manual to divine its operation. 1987 sterling 825sl / 1987 Sterling 825SL / Aaron Kiley / Car and Driver We did find two less than satisfactory items inside the Sterling. First, the hood release is located way over on the right side of the passenger's footwell. This makes sense in the British (right-hand-drive) model, but the release should have been moved for the U.S. edition. On the other hand, it wouldn't be a thoroughly British car without at least one quirk, right? The second item is the power seat controls. They're tiny arrow-shaped push buttons mounted on the center console at about mid-thigh, and they're difficult to see and almost impossible to use on the run. Lock in the memory, though, and you can set them once and forget them. Those two failings aside, the Sterling's interior is a model of good luxury design. Like true cool, true luxury is subtle, and the Sterling does a terrific job of underplaying its luxo trappings. Unlike some domestic luxury-car makers, ARG hasn't overbaked its product or compromised its essential purpose as a driving machine. All the controls and luxoid wickerbills are cleverly integrated, and they work with the delicate precision we've come to associate with—well, Honda. With the possible ex­ception of the Concorde, no machine so nicely put together has ever before come out of England. Since the Sterling line will be sold in Eu­rope as the Rover 800 series, ARG did some suspension tweaking to satisfy the higher performance expectations of the average Euro luxury-saloon buyer. The front suspension has higher-rate coil springs than the Acura and fairly long bump rubbers (ARG calls them micro­cellular polyurethane spring aids ). The coils have a linear rate; the bumpers, which come into play at the upper limits of suspension travel, get progressively stiffer as they're compressed. In comparison, the Legend uses more conventional progres­sive-rate springs. At the rear, the pivot points of the Sterling's lateral links are equipped with offset bushings, which al­low a small amount of toe-in during cor­nering and under hard braking. 1987 sterling 825sl / 1987 Sterling 825SL / Aaron Kiley / Car and Driver The sum total of these adjustments doesn't translate into much of a perfor­mance gain, at least in everyday driving. You do sense a firmer ride in the straight ­ahead mode, a bit more precision in tran­sient response, and less understeer during high-anxiety cornering, but nothing that would cause your attack hormones to go on full alert. This is not to say that the Sterling isn't fun to drive. It is, but it's adult fun rather than wild-eyed teen fun; it's the difference between watching your pal spurt beer out of his nose in the mid­dle of a joke and hearing some urbane guy make a droll pun about the Reagan ad­ministration's natural-gas policy. In the Sterling as in the Legend, beer spurts would spoil the picture. The Sterling exudes competence and refinement. It of­fers subtle satisfaction and all the right tactile cues that a mature, skillful driver should appreciate. It's still a plush cruiser, though; when pushed to the outer limits on a squiggly road, it will remind you that if burled walnut was meant to travel that fast, it wouldn't be used for china cabinets. If there is a banzai Q-ship lurking under the skin—and we think there might be—the Honda and ARG development guys have yet to uncover it. 1987 sterling 825sl / 1987 Sterling 825SL / Aaron Kiley / Car and Driver Where the Sterling is really at its best is on the superslab. At 100-plus mph it cooks along effortlessly for as long as your courage and the cops allow. You don't get much wind noise or crosswind buffeting, you're exquisitely comfortable, and you feel securely in touch with the earth. In this environment, the Sterling performs noticeably better than the Legend. On the skidpad the Sterling clocked a respectable 0.76 g, while the last Legend we tested (C/D, August 1986) went around at 0.73 g. The 825SL is equipped with 195/65VR-15 Goodyear NCT rubber, while the Legend rides on 205/60VR-15 Michelin MXVs, so the principal cause of the difference must be the Ster­ling's more neutral handling balance. 1987 sterling 825sl / 1987 Sterling 825SL / Aaron Kiley / Car and Driver It's only natural that, with its Bosch ABS II braking system, the Sterling stops bet­ter than the Legend: 183 feet versus 201. And, since it weighs 117 pounds more than the Acura, it's only natural that the British edition is slower to 60 mph: 8.5 seconds versus 8.1. Unless a fifth wheel is part of your standard driving gear, though, you won't notice the acceleration difference. Braking distances, of course, are another matter—especially when bad weather turns the pavement to Teflon. During that twelve-hour marathon from Vermont to Ann Arbor, we had the chance to test the ABS II in some awful rain. One solid application of such a sys­tem on slick pavement should be enough to convince anybody that ABS is essential equipment. 1987 sterling 825sl / 1987 Sterling 825SL / Aaron Kiley / Car and Driver ARG's reentry into our market is off to a good start. The Sterling is not only a high­-quality piece but, considering its British origins (with a little help from Tokyo), a pleasant surprise. The Sterling is sched­uled to go on sale in January at 145 deal­ers around the country, and ARCONA ex­pects to sell around 30,000 copies during 1987. As the dealers like to say, they're priced to move. The base price for the 825S is $19,000—about $800 cheaper than the base Legend—and the full-house 825SL will retail for $23,000. What we have here could be the beginnings of a real war. And the victor, it appears, will be the American car buyer. Counterpoints With a little help from Japan, England has just entered the modern automotive age. Front-wheel drive! A multivalve V-6 engine! Electronic fuel injection! And all this technology is nicely bal­anced against the Old World verities of leather and wood. The third time may be the charm for Rover product in America. You can always tell when a car compa­ny is striving to establish itself. In the case of Austin Rover, the try-hard atti­tude is evident in the Sterling's base price and long standard-equipment list. One-upping Saab, Volvo, BMW, and Audi (and even partner Honda) is no small undertaking, so ARG is riding into town with six-shooters blazing. The 825SL we tested is a creditable effort. Its styling is perhaps ten times more memorable than the Legend's. Its ride isn't quite as plush, but it feels firm­er when the going gets tough. Its instru­ment panel is no better or worse than its Japanese sister's. I do have a couple of minor complaints—the steering-col­umn stalks feel rubbery, and the seat controls are ergonomically correct only for those of smaller stature—but you have my blessing to add one more automobile to your shopping list. —Don Sherman I once knew a wizened little white-haired photographer who, when things were going badly, would mutter, Well, that's just sterling, Moss! I always figured the great British driver would get a kick out of this wee colonist's twisting of his name. But I wonder what Stirl and his countrymen think of the English charac­ter that's been twisted into this Sterling—which is, after all, no more than a Legend (which is simply a glori­fied Honda) wrapped in a Union Jack. Actually, wrapped is the wrong word. The Sterling bears no vestige of the cardigan-sweater look that once put the frump in British bodies. This sedan bypasses the Japanese Legend's bland bulbousness, dovetailing a crisp aero look with roomy packaging efficiency. Though a few joints inside are less than jig true, the well-polished harvest of a British forest finishes off a warmly pleasing cabin. The Sterling's cozy cab­in, however, suggests what its chassis confirms: the more sportingly trimmed and tuned Legend is a bit livelier to drive. The difference is just Sterling, Moss. —Larry Griffin Ten to fifteen years ago, Japanese cars were already known for being reliable and cheap, but their styling and road manners attracted few admirers. A little British influence in those two areas could have worked wonders then. Comparing the Legend and the Ster­ling, however, I'm not sure that the Brit­ish influence is an unqualified blessing these days. In its favor, the Sterling has cleaner and smoother lines than the Legend. I prefer the cozier flavor of its wood-and-leather interior, too, but the Legend's instrument cluster is cleaner and its controls are more logically ar­ranged. On the road, the Legend has the softer ride, while the Sterling's more neutral handling and ABS give it the edge in quick going. On the whole, the two cars' advan­tages and disadvantages balance out rather evenly. The fact that I can't choose between the two flavors of the same basic car tells me that the Japanese are likely to move into the prestige-car business with their usual success in the near future. —Csaba Csere Specifications Specifications 1987 Sterling 825SL Vehicle Type: front-engine, front-wheel-drive, 5-passenger, 4-door sedan PRICE As Tested: $24,000 (est) ENGINE SOHC 24-valve V-6, aluminum block and heads, port fuel injection Displacement: 152 in 3, 2494 cm Power: 151 hp @ 5800 rpm Torque: 154 lb-ft @ 4500 rpm TRANSMISSION 5-speed manual CHASSIS Suspension, F/R: control arms/multilink Brakes, F/R: 11.2-in vented disc/10.3-in disc Tires: Goodyear Eagle NCT65 195/65VR-15 DIMENSIONS Wheelbase: 108.6 in Length: 188.8 in Width: 68.1 in Height: 54.8 in Passenger Volume, F/R: 49/42 ft Trunk Volume: 12 ft Curb Weight: 3277 lb C/D TEST RESULTS 60 mph: 8.5 sec 1/4-Mile: 16.5 sec @ 84 mph 100 mph: 27.0 sec Top Gear, 30–50 mph: 12.8 sec Top Gear, 50–70 mph: 15.0 sec Top Speed: 131 mph Braking, 70–0 mph: 183 ft Roadholding, 300-ft Skidpad: 0.76 g Interior Sound Idle: 41 dBA Full Throttle: 77 dBA 70-mph Cruising: 71 dBA C/D FUEL ECONOMY Observed: 19 mpg

Patents provide a glimpse into what automaker R&D teams are working on, and are not always visions of the future. But this one makes sense: by moving powertrain components away from the flat, skateboard battery design and either into a tunnel between the front seats or in the front or rear of the pack, designers and engineers will have more freedom, potentially bringing EV power to more body styles. More interior room is also an option with a thinner pack down below. Toyota has published two related patents that could make its upcoming EVs feel more spacious inside or improve handling by rearranging powertrain components. The patents, first spotted by Autoblog, describe an electric vehicle with two rows of seats—where the second-row seat is located rearward of the first-row seat. Isn't patent-ese great?—and that has two integrated electromechanical units that are each made up of a motor and an electric power controller. Rather than directly connecting some powertrain components like power control units and junction boxes to the large battery under the seats, the components would be installed in two electromechanical units located either in front of or behind the battery (patent #20260021715) or in a center tunnel that is arranged between the two front seats (#20260021716). Toyota Vehicles For Sale Near You See all results for used Toyota for sale near 31707 The two patents were originally filed in the summer of 2025 and published in January 2026. More powertrain design flexibility could also mean Toyota's EV setup could be used in less-tall vehicles, potentially bringing plug-in power to sedans or hatchbacks. Moving powertrain components away from the battery could also have safety implications. toyota ev battery packaging patent / U.S. Patent and Trademark Office One of the patents (as shown in the image above) imagines putting a plurality of the EV's battery-related devices below the second-row seat and above the battery, and above the second integrated electromechanical unit. Moving these components also changes the vehicle's weight distribution, which could be impactful when adjusting how a future Toyota EV that uses these patents might feel on the road. Changing where components sit can also shorten the length of cables needed to power the vehicle, which could save weight and space while improving efficiency. Toyota already has experience using two motors in an EV to deliver enhanced performance. The bZ Woodland, for example, comes standard with a dual-motor, all-wheel-drive powertrain that produces 375 horsepower. Toyota did not respond to our request for comment on these patents. toyota patent diagram of a manual gear shift simulator for an ev and diagrams illustrating a mechanical system and torque vs rotation speed graph / Toyota patent diagram of a manual gear shift simulator for an EV / United States Patent and Trademark Office Additionally, one of the patents includes details related to a manual gear shift simulator for an electric vehicle. From what we can gather, the details of this technology appear to match the setup that Toyota let us try out a few years ago in a battery-powered Lexus UX. As with all such work, simply because an automaker files for a patent on a specific technology does not mean it will bring that technology to future vehicles. Patents are a way for inventors and companies to protect inventions and explore ideas that might not ever reach production. But patents can provide those of us on outside world with insight into what companies like Toyota are working on deep in their R&D offices. For example, the Toyota engineer who submitted the patents discussed above, Nanae Iwasaki, is also listed as inventor on a patent that describes a two-motor EV that decides between charge modes from external power sources based on the temperature levels of the two motors and two inverters in the vehicle. That patent was published in September 2024. ➡️ Skip the lot. Let Car and Driver help you find your next car. Headshot of Sebastian Blanco Contributing Editor Sebastian Blanco has been writing about electric vehicles, hybrids, and hydrogen cars since 2006. His articles and car reviews have appeared in the New York Times, Automotive News, Reuters, SAE, Autoblog, InsideEVs, Trucks.com, Car Talk, and other outlets. His first green-car media event was the launch of the Tesla Roadster, and since then he has been tracking the shift away from gasoline-powered vehicles and discovering the new technology's importance not just for the auto industry, but for the world as a whole. Throw in the recent shift to autonomous vehicles, and there are more interesting changes happening now than most people can wrap their heads around. You can find him on Twitter or, on good days, behind the wheel of a new EV.

-Limited hybrid readouts -Price keeps creeping up -Not everyone loves the post-refresh front end ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW Since it burst onto the scene in 2021, the Ford Maverick has gotten pretty much everything right, executing its mission—be cheap, cheerful, and useful—without any of the tired posturing that dominates the pickup truck space. It's simply a good, honest truck, one so good out of the gate that it finished a single vote shy of claiming our 2022 Truck of the Year award—and then it A thorough and substantive refresh powered this year's TOTY win, including finally making AWD available with its frugal hybrid powertrain. The addition isn't just a tweak—it's a fundamental correction that finally makes the Maverick lineup feel whole. (In fact, the Maverick might have been a two-time Truck of the Year winner had this version been available from the jump.) ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW Same Power, Better Reality The AWD hybrid's combined 191 horsepower is the exact same as produced by front-drive hybrid Mavericks. Yet the truck drives with a noticeable difference from the first time you step on the gas with gusto. Our primary observations during regular driving is that it's more stable and feels quicker overall than the front-drive model. All-wheel drive allows the hybrid system to more effectively deploy its minimal amount of might, especially when merging after a cloverleaf, accelerating over loose surfaces, or pulling away from a stop on slippery pavement. Our testing backs up that impression on a closed course, too, even if the stopwatch doesn't reveal a massive delta. The Maverick AWD Hybrid runs from 0 to 60 mph in 7.0 seconds, 0.7 quicker than the front-drive hybrid ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW Like every Maverick we've driven, this version is a better-handling truck than it has any right to be. Its steering is light but accurate, body motions are well controlled, and the overall experience remains more carlike than truckish. You don't need the (also new) Lobo trim to have fun driving a Maverick (although we recommend at least test-driving that one because it's a blast). Switch into Sport mode and turn off traction control, and the hybrid AWD Maverick will even let you have some fun on dirt—we had a blast sliding it through dusty curves. In regular driving, this easy controllability translates to sure-footed confidence in all kinds of weather and on various surfaces. We also spent half a day off-roading the AWD Maverick Hybrid, and you might be surprised by what it can do. With momentum and patience, it will climb and scramble over terrain that can strand larger and theoretically more capable trucks. The Maverick's off-road performance is limited by ground clearance, tire choice, and cooling, not will. ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW The AWD Maverick Hybrid even tows well, and you can now double its tugging capacity to 4,000 pounds with the 4K Tow package. We didn't tow that much weight, but we did pull 1,500 pounds' worth of jet skis, and the truck drove as if they weren't even there. Tow/Haul mode is programmed well, igniting the engine whenever you move away from a stop (the hybrid will often start and cruise in electric-only mode) and increasing regenerative braking when you lift off the gas. I've personally towed in larger trucks that didn't tow this well even at half or less of their capacity. Interior and Tech: Better, Still Honest Ford wisely resisted the urge to fuss too much with the Maverick's interior for the recent update, which remains a masterclass in practicality and visual interest despite an abundance of low-cost materials. A new, larger infotainment screen replaces what was a largely useless cubbyhole and modernizes the cabin without impacting the Maverick's clever charm. The carved soapstone-look trim remains, and the varied textures still do an excellent job masking the truck's affordable roots. Nearly five years in, it's still one of the smartest interiors out there—not because it's fancy, but because it's thoughtful. We still love the storage under the rear seat, the door cutouts that hold large water bottles, and the splashes of color throughout. That said, there are some misses. You can't get adaptive cruise control unless you spring for the top-shelf versions, and we'd really like to have more insight into the hybrid powertrain's energy flow and performance data. These are nitpicks, not dealbreakers. The Verdict The Ford Maverick AWD Hybrid doesn't alter what the Maverick is—it completes it. From daily commuting and snowy driveways to towing small toys and dirt-road detours, this is the do-everything configuration the lineup has been missing, and it still returns excellent fuel economy at 40/34/37 mpg city/highway/combined. The Maverick was already one of the smartest trucks you could buy. Now it's also one of the most versatile. -Barely quicker than before -Even heavier than before -Optioned up price higher than before ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW When we last tested the luxurious Mercedes-Benz EQS450+, back when it was a 2022 MotorTrend Car of the Year finalist, the big electric sedan struck us as a luxurious contrarian. It was unapologetically comfort forward, with a wind-carved silhouette some embraced as futuristic, but others dismissed as an exercise in soap-bar smooth anonymity. The EQS acted like a Mercedes first and an EV second, an electrified spacecraft of an S-Class that made silence, range, and eye-catching strangeness part of its charm. Not blindingly quick or super sporty, but deeply soothing. Bold, in its own sort of way. Since that First Test, the EQS has been quietly, steadily updated. Power has gradually nudged upward to 355 hp and 419 lb-ft for the rear-drive-based model we just had in for testing. Mercedes also upsized the car's battery to 118 kWh and layered in new regeneration logic to provide smoother, stronger deceleration. And now, for the 2026 model year ( likely the car's U.S. swan song for now), a NACS fast-charging adapter comes standard. ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW So, does this massaged EQS now stand a better chance against the rival that beat it back in 2022, the Lucid Air? That's what we're here to find out. Range Gains The little + on the EQS450+ still means what it always has: maximum range. It started strong when it first launched at 350 EPA-rated miles, and the new battery bumps the 2026 model's estimate to 390 miles—a gain that checks out in the real world. In our MotorTrend Road-Trip Range test, where we run an EV from 100 percent to 5 percent charge at a steady 70 mph, the EQS450+ went 392 miles before easing to a halt, quietly exceeding the promise on its sticker. ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW That puts the big Benz much closer to the Lucid Air Pure RWD's 420-mile EPA rating. The rear-drive Pure, newer to the scene and a more natural rival than the AWD Lucid we tested for 2022 Car of the Year, still goes farther on a charge. But the gap has tightened, and the EQS450+ now feels like a more legitimate contender in the long-range, luxury electric sedan arena. Packing on Those Pounds Thanks primarily to the extra power onboard, the EQS 450+ is a touch quicker than before, emphasis on touch. Our latest numbers show an improvement of roughly 0.1 second in both its 0⁠–⁠60-mph run (5.3 seconds) and the quarter mile (13.9 seconds at 102.1 mph). The gains are modest in part because this EQS weighs 131 pounds more than ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW The scale tilts even harder when you compare it against the 2025 Lucid Air Pure RWD, which is way, way lighter than the Mercedes (a full 1,063 pounds) and pairs that with a stronger output of 430 hp and 406 lb-ft. The result is predictable but still stark: The Air blasts to 60 mph in 4.5 seconds and clears the quarter in 12.8. Given its more relaxed pace in a straight line, the EQS450+ prefers an unbroken flow to any sort of fireworks. Its power delivery is calm, clean, and entirely sufficient for everyday driving. Sure, rivals like the Lucid are quicker, but the Mercedes never feels sluggish. Slip it into Sport, and the throttle wakes up, yet even in Comfort it gathers speed with an effortless, almost liquid ease. The EQS isn't a lap time chaser, but on the highway, it rarely left us wanting for pace. Throwing Its Weight Around Acceleration isn't the only place you feel the mass of the EQS 450+. Push it through a corner with any real intent, and the body will lean as the car's weight shifts across its vast footprint. Dial braking regeneration down to its lightest setting, and the friction brakes step forward, working harder to manage all that momentum. Even so, the car's test numbers hardly budge. The EQS stops from 60 mph in the same distance as before and only a foot longer than the Air Pure's impressive 111 feet. It also shrugged off repeated 100-mph panic stops without drama. The pedal is short, sturdy, and confidence-building. Get too close to the system's limits, though, and we found the ABS is eager to chime in, as it did for us. ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW The steering is feather-light but reassuringly precise, and its rear-wheel steering gives this big sedan unexpected agility. Paired with ample grip, it turns more tightly and neatly than its size suggests. Its skidpad and figure-eight numbers don't meaningfully improve over the 2022 car (a 0.91 g average on the pad and 26.0 seconds at 0.70 g average through the figure eight), but they remain respectable. Still, the Lucid Air Pure stays comfortably ahead at 0.95 g and 24.5 seconds at 0.80 g. The Fancy Get Fancier The EQS 450+ remains every bit the stately cruiser it was when we first tested it, but the 2026 iteration leans even harder into its premium identity, the S(-Class) in EQS. Standard upgrades introduced last year carry forward, including headrest pillows, softer rear cushions, and some formerly optional amenities (namely soft-close doors and a digital key). Last year also brought the standard Luxury Line appearance package with its model-specific grille, AMG Line bumpers, and a proper standing hood star (ironically invisible from the driver's seat). For 2026, Mercedes added two new ambient Sound Experiences, Fractal Fusion and the wonderfully odd Granular Fuzz, layering a bit more theatricality onto an already lavish cabin. The EQS' cabin continues to feel like a rolling high-end lounge. The seats, front and rear, are beautifully sculpted and fully perforated, with massaging capability throughout the cabin. A dedicated passenger display hovers in front, while the two-tier center console offers clever storage, including an elastic strap on the lower shelf to corral loose items. In back, a removable tablet governed the rear seats of our test model, in addition to ambient lighting and an expansive sunroof, supported by a built-in phone charger. A four-passenger layout and a generous hatchback cargo area with underfloor storage round out the blend of indulgence and practicality. ADVERTISEMENT - CONTINUE READING BELOW The exterior feels equally considered. Our test car's Manufaktur China Blue paint, a $6,500 flourish, pairs with additional Manufaktur touches to give the EQS a quiet, confident presence. Rear puddle lights and LED-fringed pop-out door handles act like a soft spotlight as you approach. The result is a flagship EV that doesn't shout its luxury so much as it layers it on in a carefully considered way. The Bottom Line Surprisingly, the base price of the EQS450+ has come down since 2022 (it was $103,360), but after all the add-ons to our Mercedes, our as-tested price is some $18,015 more than our previous tester. Critically, it's also some $54,000 more than a fully loaded 2026 Air Pure. After the incremental updates, the EQS450+ has improved but in many ways is still fundamentally the same car we tested in 2022. Range is still a real strength, the steering is pleasantly direct, and the ride remains comfortably compliant. The interior upgrades sharpen its premium feel, and day-to-day drivability is as easygoing as ever. Yet the EQS also carries more weight than before. As a result, its acceleration gains are negligible, and it continues to trail the Lucid Air on outright performance and driving engagement. The four-passenger layout further narrows its appeal; option it up, and it can get very expensive. For buyers who prioritize comfort and quiet above speed and athleticism, the EQS450+ will still make sense. Its longer range and polished cabin help keep it competitive, but they don't fundamentally shift its position in the segment. Against a strong rival like the Lucid Air, the EQS is the more relaxed, less dynamic choice. The updates make it better, but not enough to change its core character or its standing in the luxury EV hierarchy. These days, everyone is looking for a deal. And rightfully so: Everything feels more expensive. Perhaps it's reality, perhaps it is perception. Either way, couponing and Grouponing are popular. Generally, all parties in a coupon situation are privy to what's going down: A customer is lured in by sale pricing in the hopes they'll either spend more or become return customers. For Plant City, Florida's Mozalez Auto Repair (@mozalez_autorepair), it had been a winning formula. Yet recently, Mark Morales, the owner of Mozales Auto Repair, took to TikTok to vent about a particularly problematic customer and to explain why he shut down Groupon. As Morales explains, he initially accepted Groupon because it brought in customers. But there was a downside since it brought in customers he viewed as difficult and overly price-sensitive. He explained this using language that drew notice in the comments. I just had a situation, he says. A girl comes up, 'Hey, my mom, she bought a Groupon. But like any coupon, the Groupon has boundaries. In this case, the oil change on offer is for up to five quarts of oil. After that, the customer is responsible for the remaining oil, at a cost of $10 per quart. The vehicle that purportedly requires service is a 5.3L Chevrolet Suburban. According to the mechanic, if that model vehicle is newer than 2017, it requires almost eight quarts of oil. That's three quarts of oil at full price. Morales explains this to the customer. Now she's got to call her mother. That's where things get really unpleasant. During the call, after the mechanic has explained things, the mother starts cursing. She calls him a mother[expletive] who's just trying to get over on somebody and make more money. He alleges that she ends the call saying, Just take the money from Groupon and do your mother[expletive] job. The mechanic is taken aback, and the customer is mortified. She says she'll cover the cost herself. 'Thank You; No Thank You.' But by now, Morales has come to his own decision and declines to service the vehicle. Thank you; no thank you. I don't need your money, he says. The kicker? Morales alleges that this woman (the mother) has already tried these bullying tactics. This isn't the first time this [has happened], he says. She's done it before. The internet overwhelmingly supports the mechanic. One account, NickOfTimeAuto (@nickoftimeauto), that seems familiar with Mozalez Auto Repair says, I told you man, you don't want the bottom feeder customers that the majority of those Groupons and specials bring. I'm not just talking, 25 years in business and booked 7-10 days out I'm telling you these people shouldn't be your focus. Keep doing what you are doing and eventually your referral and repeat business will give you more then you need. The user, A Glimpse Into My Life (@skylarsbackyard), offers a little commiserating perspective: No one ever reads anything. I deal with this all day. My favorite is when they say you have no idea who I am!!! No sir I don't know who you are but I don't have to worry about who you are if you throwing a fit over $35. However, one attentive user does ask a sociologically inflected question: Good story but what type of people are 'ghetto' just curious, writes Big_jefe85 (@big_jefe85). What To Do If You Feel Like You've Been Bait-And-Switched It can feel—for everyone—that we're living through the scammiest of times. A generous reading of the situation would suggest that both the mechanic and his potential customer have been in situations where they came out feeling taken advantage of. The site, My Florida Legal, reports, Auto repair rip-offs consistently rank as one of the most common categories of consumer complaints throughout the nation. To avoid opportunistic service, the site suggests shopping around, getting a quote before any work is done, cross-checking any warranties on your vehicle, and getting a copy of the work done on your vehicle. After the repair is completed, ask about parts and labor warranties. Finally, in the case that you think you've been cheated, you can file a complaint with your state's appropriate consumer services bureau. Failing that, there are internet reviews. In this situation, the oil change was not operationally critical and was likely easy to resolve. But it does illustrate the frustration and fear that unsavory encounters sow on both sides: the customer and the purveyor. For his part, Mark Morales told Motor1 that he's not sure what happened with the Suburban that needed an oil change because the vehicle's owner hung up on him. But, I have not heard from them or Groupon or seen any bad reviews, he wrote in a text message. As for his relationship with Groupon, though he's submitted to stop the campaign, it's still running. So he's price-matching it for anyone who comes in. Fair. We want your opinion! What would you like to see on Motor1.com? Take our 3 minute survey. - The Motor1.com Team

The Breakdown: Ford Mustang sales were up 50.4 percent last month compared to January 2025. The Mustang outsold the Mach-E by a wide margin. Ford ended 2025 strong with Mustang sales up 3.0 percent after a slow start. After a shaky start to 2025, Ford Mustang sales ended on a high note that's already continuing into the new year. The Dearborn-based automaker sold more Mustangs in January 2025 than it did one year prior, with sales up 50.4 percent. Ford sold 3,609 Mustangs in January, which outsold the Mach-E by a wide margin. The automaker sold only 1,040 examples of its electric crossover, with sales down a whopping 70.5 percent since the federal incentives ended. The Mach-E outsold the Mustang last year, but we don't expect that to happen in 2026. Mustang sales got off to a very slow start in 2025, down 31.6 percent through the first three months. Sales were up 3.2 percent in the second quarter and then tumbled 10 percent in the quarter after. It wasn't until the fourth quarter that sales figures truly started to show signs of a rebound. Jan 2026 Sales Jane 2025 Sales % Change Mustang Mustang Mach-E Ford ended last year selling 12,515 Mustangs in just three months, an increase of 66.5 percent that helped the model overcome a 10.1 percent sales deficit in 2024. Mustang sales ended up 3.0 percent for 2025, but it's too early to know for sure if Ford will repeat that success for 2026. While Mustang sales are trending upward, they still remain well below their pre-pandemic levels. Ford sold just over 72,000 Mustangs in 2019, down from 122,000 in 2015 with the launch of the S550. Mustang sales have never been the same after the mid-2000s and the Great Recession; the company sold 166,530 units in 2006. 2026 Ford Mustang FX Package Photo by: Ford Motor1's Take: The Mustang might still be America's best-selling sports car, with sales finally trending upward, but the car still has fewer buyers than ever. Consumers continue to buy crossovers, trucks, and SUVs but are unable or unwilling to add a Mustang—or other sports cars—to their garages, as sales across the segment are trending downward. This is a good start for the Mustang, but we don't expect it to be a banner year for the model. We want your opinion! What would you like to see on Motor1.com? If you've ever watched a video, read an article, or merely listened to a car nerd rant about cars, you've probably heard these terms before: turbocharged, supercharged, and naturally aspirated. Given context, you probably know they have something to do with the engine and, on vibes alone, turbo- and supercharging probably make the car go faster somehow. But what do these words actually mean? We're here to demystify. Naturally Aspirated 2023 Chevrolet Corvette Z06 Flat Plane Crank V8 Chevrolet Corvette Z06's Flat-Plane Crank V8 When someone refers to a gas-burning engine as naturally aspirated, this simply means that there are no turbochargers or superchargers involved (those two things, by the way, are collectively known as forced induction ). That's it. The absence of forced induction is the definition. This means that air and gas mix, causing little explosions inside cylinders, which in turn cause pistons to move up and down (or sometimes side to side) rapidly, rotating a crankshaft that turns the wheels. That's the high-level explanation of how a car's gas engine works. More simply: gas and air go boom, causing cylinder movement, which causes crankshaft rotation, moving the car forward. And a naturally aspirated engine simply refers to an engine where the boom-creation step isn't assisted by any sort of fan or air-based trickery. When someone refers to a gas-burning engine as 'naturally aspirated,' this simply means that there are no turbochargers or superchargers involved. That's it. As a driver, natural aspiration typically means a more linear throttle response, meaning the correlation between how deeply your foot is buried in the gas pedal and how quickly the engine is spinning feels like it's a straight line. With turbos, this line often feels more curved, making for a less predictable and arguably less organic-feeling driving experience. The big driving drawback with NA, though, particularly with the most common small-displacement engines, is that you have to rev them out hard to get them to do anything exciting. This has its charms, but it isn't very fuel-efficient and usually not ideal if all you want out of your car is pure speed. Within certain enthusiast circles, however, naturally aspirated cars now carry more reverence because of their purer character and image—think cooking on cast iron rather than stainless steel or non-stick. NA is also a rarity these days among new cars, which definitely has something to do with it. If your car was made before, say, 2012 and wasn't specifically marketed as a turbocharged, go-faster version, chances are, your engine is naturally aspirated. Over the past decade, however, turbocharging has taken over en masse. Turbocharging Toyota GR Corolla Three-Cylinder Engine Toyota GR Corolla's Turbocharged Three-Cylinder Engine Photo by: Toyota A turbocharger is a physical part that sort of looks like a metal snail with a fan in it. It takes exhaust gases that would otherwise be expelled unceremoniously and uses them to spin a fan called a turbine wheel, which in turn spins a second fan called the compressor wheel. As driving machines, older turbocharged cars were largely characterized by something called turbo lag. Old turbochargers took a while to spool up, and before they did, the engine would feel relatively gutless. But once boost was built, torque would spike fast. This sudden spike in torque was polarizing at best, theatrical in hindsight, and, if we're honest with ourselves, a little scary mid-corner. Modern turbocharged cars, however, are almost the opposite. Thanks to developments like smaller, quicker-spooling turbos, direct fuel injection, and perhaps even hybrid electric motor-spooled turbos, torque comes on pretty much immediately and stays there. Little to no lag, no sudden spike, no surprises. This results in a driving character that's capable no matter where you are in the rev range—step on it and it just goes—but a little boringly so. Other compromises include increased manufacturing cost and compromised reliability inherent with complicating anything. Modern turbos are getting cheaper and more robust every day, sure, simply because that's how scale works. But everything else being equal, expect an engine with more parts, more heat, more pressure, and more points of failure to cost more to buy and maintain than one without. Despite that, turbocharging is basically ubiquitous among new vehicles now, whether you're talking low-slung supercars, full-size pickup trucks, or small economy hatchbacks. In 2026, a turbocharged four-cylinder has become the standard-issue, regular-person-car engine. Supercharging 2025 Ford Mustang GTD The Ford Mustang GTD's Supercharged V8 Superchargers, meanwhile, aren't nearly as common as turbochargers but operate with a similar goal: create more power by compressing more air into the engine. The way it goes about this mission, however, is more mechanical in nature. A bit like a turbo, superchargers (some people like to call them blowers ) take ambient air from outside and suck it into the engine for more intense combustion. But instead of exhaust gases doing the spinning, it's a pulley system attached to the engine's crankshaft. Because superchargers are powered by engine movement itself rather than downstream fumes, the additional boost they provide is practically immediate. This means more thrust pretty much regardless of engine speed—roots and twin-screw blowers don't so much alter how a naturally aspirated engine's torque curve is shaped, it simply makes it sit higher on the graph. Because superchargers use the engine to power themselves, their big caveat is worse fuel economy. This parasitic loss means superchargers are pretty much purely a performance play, not an efficiency one. The supercharger's most distinctive driving trait, however, might just be the noise they make. It's a high-pitched whine that'll sound familiar to anybody who's ever heard a Dodge Hellcat fly by. It's a deeply mechanical, primal, borderline unhinged sound, and is a big reason for its popularity with American muscle and muscle-adjacent genres of automobile. You know that thing that intimidatingly sticks out of, say, the hood of Dominic Toretto's Charger and has three air holes and a pulley system peeking out? That's a supercharger. We want your opinion! Americans love pickup trucks. But so does the rest of the world. Granted, they don't embrace the full-size and heavy-duty pickups as we do, but this past year has been an exciting one for truck enthusiasts outside of the United States. No, you won't find the Ford F-150, Chevrolet Silverado 1500, or Ram 1500 atop best-seller lists globally but other regions still buy pickups—and lots of them. The Ford Ranger dominates in Europe, while theToyota Hilux maintains a stronghold in Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. Even Australia is ripe with all manner of interesting pickup. Even though we're spoiled for choice, it's hard not to get a little jealous of all the trucks we can't have. These are some of the newest non-US pickups that launched last year. Which one would you want? Dacia Duster Truck Dacia Duster pickup truck Photo by: Dacia Dacia had to outsource this to a local coachbuilder, but it's technically a new pickup truck option we can't have in the United States. It's based on the compact Dacia Duster and has a super-tiny bed that measures 41.3 inches (1,050 millimeters) long and 39.3 inches (1,000 millimeters) wide. What makes it so adorable is the fact that the design remains largely unchanged. The rear doors are the same as before, with all the major modifications occurring at the back, which keeps costs down. Despite the bed's diminutive size, the Duster truck can carry up to 948 pounds of cargo. That won't compete with an F-150 by any stretch, but it's an option we don't get here that seems like the type of vehicle that could easily create a cult following. Ford Ranger Super Duty 2026 Ford Ranger Super Duty (Australia) Photo by: Ford While Ford offers the Ranger and Ranger Raptor in the United States, we don't get every flavor, including this new Super Duty trim that would put the F-150 to shame. The Ranger SD can tow up to 9,921 lbs, 1,700 more than Ford's full-size truck. Under the hood is an upgraded 3.0-liter turbodiesel, producing 207 horsepower and 443 pound-feet of torque, paired to a 10-speed automatic transmission. The 33-inch wheels wrap around wheels with eight studs. It has a hood-mounted snorkel, and the Ranger's optional 34-gallon fuel tank is standard in the Super Duty. The truck, which is only available in Australia, comes in multiple configurations, single-cab chassis and double-cab pickup. Kia Tasman Base 2025 Kia Tasman work truck Photo by: Kia The Kia Tasman is the Korean automaker's rival to the Ford Ranger and Toyota Hilux. Like other Kias, it wears a very distinctive design, with a unique grille and headlights pushed to the sides, sitting in front of the blocky plastic fender flares. It's a unique look that's available in single-, double-, and chassis-cab configurations. The interior isn't as rugged-looking as its exterior, with a fairly standard modern cabin, but Kia claims its truck has class-leading headroom, shoulder room, and second-row legroom. It has three screens—two 12.3-inch displays and a 5.0-inch one—physical controls, reclining rear seats, and a folding console table. Depending on the market, Kia offers the Tasman with a six-speed manual gearbox or an eight-speed automatic transmission. Powertrains also depend on the market, with a 277-hp 2.5-liter gas engine or a 207-hp 2.2-liter turbodiesel available. The Tasman went on sale in the first half of 2025. Nissan Frontier Pro Plug-In Hybrid Nissan Frontier Pro (China) Photo by: Nissan The Nissan Frontier Pro sounds like something that should be sold in America, but it's not. The Pro is Nissan's first electrified pickup, a plug-in hybrid, and it's based on the Nissan Dongfeng Z9. Despite hybrid vehicles gaining popularity, the Frontier Pro is not coming here. Powering the pickup is a 1.5-liter four-cylinder with an electric motor in the transmission. The powertrain produces a combined 402 hp and 590 lb-ft of torque. It comes with manually selectable 4High and 4Low modes. It also has Pure Electric, Hybrid, Performance, and Snow modes. The cabin looks like any other modern vehicle with a small 10.0-inch driver display, a 14.6-inch screen on the dash, and a row of real buttons below the center vents. While the Frontier Pro PHEV is a Chinese-based vehicle, Nissan plans to sell it in other markets—just not America. Nissan Navara 2026 Nissan Navara Photo by: Nissan Nissan launched the new Navara last year, and it's heavily related to the Mitsubishi Triton. It looks tough, and higher-end trims like the ST-X and Pro-4X grades come with the brand's Super 4WD, which features a Torsen limited-slip differential. The automaker will offer the Navara in Australia with a 2.4-liter turbodiesel engine, producing 201 hp and 347 lb-ft of torque. It has seven drive modes: Normal, Eco, Gravel, Snow, Mud, Sand, and Rock. Inside, the Triton's influences are clearly visible, with only the Nissan badge on the steering wheel standing out as the most significant change. It has a 9.0-inch infotainment screen and a 7.0-inch driver's display. Toyota Hilux Electric 2026 Toyota Hilux Photo by: Toyota Toyota unveiled the ninth-generation Hilux last year and developed a fully electric version for the first time. The EV has two electric motors and a 59.2-kilowatt-hour lithium-ion battery pack. The automaker will also offer a 2.8-liter mild-hybrid turbodiesel, a 2.8-liter diesel, and a 2.7-liter gasoline engine, depending on the market. In 2028, Toyota will offer a fuel cell version. Inside, the Hilux retains physical controls and includes a driver's display and a 12.3-inch infotainment screen with over-the-air updates. Toyota sells the Hilux in over 180 markets worldwide, and America will never be one of them. Ram Rampage 2026 Ram Rampage (European version) Photo by: Ram The Ram Rampage is now heading to Europe after debuting in Brazil a few years ago. It's a unibody pick that Ram will offer in Rebel and R/T trims. The Rebel is powered by a 2.2-liter Multijet turbodiesel engine, producing 200 hp and 332 lb-ft of torque. It needs 9.9 seconds to hit 62 mph. The Rampage R/T gets a 2.0-liter Hurricane engine making 272 hp and 294 lb-ft of torque that propels the truck to 62 mph three seconds quicker. Inside, the Rampage is nice, with a 12.3-inch infotainment screen, a 10.3-inch digital instrument cluster, and an available Harman Kardon sound system. It has a max payload capacity of 2,237 lbs.
