The Breakdown: The Grand Tour returns in 2026 with three new hosts. The hosts are trainspotter Francis Bourgeois and YouTubers Thomas Holland and James Engelsman. Six new episodes will air sometime later this year. The Grand Tour lives on. Amazon Prime Video has announced three new hosts for the beloved motoring show—and it appears last year's rumors were true. Heading the next installment of the series is trainspotter and influencer Francis Bourgeois, as well as YouTubers Thomas Holland and James Engelsman. Holland and Engelsman host the Throttle House YouTube channel, which has 3.4 million subscribers. Bourgeois is the train-loving social media star with nearly 6 million followers. The trio will host six episodes, which hit the streaming service globally later this year. Holland, Engelsman, and Bourgeois will replace Jeremy Clarkson, Richard Hammond, and James May, who ended their hosting duties in September 2024. Just before the last episode aired, Amazon announced the show would continue with new hosts. Clarkson participated in the announcement, appearing in a short clip selecting the new hosts on The Grand Tour's Instagram page. Studio Lambert is producing the series. Clarkson, Hammond, and May dissolved their production company, W. Chump and Sons, after leaving the show. Holland said in the announcement, When I first heard they were rebooting The Grand Tour and replacing Clarkson, Hammond, and May, I said 'only a moron would take that job.' Motor1's Take: The Grand Tour isn't a show about cars. It's about the camaraderie they create, regardless of whether you're driving million-dollar supercars or questionable hoopties you bought for a few hundred bucks. The cars don't really matter—it's the memories, and who you make them with, that do, and that kind of natural chemistry that entices viewers is hard to create. We wish the new hosts the best of luck. Sources: The Grand Tour / Instagram,Variety We want your opinion! What would you like to see on Motor1.com? Take our 3 minute survey. - The Motor1.com Team

Last year's Super Bowl, if you can remember that far back, pitted the Philadelphia Eagles against the Kansas City Chiefs in New Orleans. Long before the matchup was cemented, Mercedes asked if I wanted to road-trip a Benz from Atlanta to New Orleans for Super Bowl weekend, and I replied that I'd make that trip for lesser reasons than that—I once drove an Aston Martin Rapide from Atlanta to New Orleans just to get a really good po' boy. As for my steed, we settled on the 831-hp Mercedes-AMG GT63 S E-Performance, a car that would soon thereafter destroy our acceleration records for non-EV four-doors, running the quarter-mile in 10.5 seconds at 131 mph. As if numbers like that weren't license-endangering enough, this example was painted a searing yellow sure to catch the eye of every cop from Georgia to Louisiana. I resolved to seek the solitude of back roads when possible. luxury sports car parked outside a building / Just a humble hatchback. My wife, Heather, was my co-pilot. I know some people's significant others tend to act as a shotgun-seat check on accelerative overexuberance, but that is not the case with Heather. This is not because she has an inner Pastrana streak, but because she'd always rather be at the destination than in the car, so knocking down the ETA is fine with her, within reason. I've been with her in a Lamborghini Urus with a colleague driving 175 mph in Europe and she didn't say anything, although later she said that's mostly because she didn't want to distract the guy behind the wheel at 175 mph. Sound logic. Any anyway, I didn't plan on doing that in the GT63 S, but if I did exploit that 831 horsepower now and then, I'd probably do it while Heather was napping. She naps a lot on road trips, her way of fast-forwarding the drive via unconsciousness. If she could be frozen in carbonite like Han Solo for a road trip, she'd do it. For Sale Near You See all results for used cars for sale near 60602 Loading up the GT63 S in Atlanta, we find that the Benz's hatchback makes it quite practical for a car that can hunt Hellcats at the drag strip. The cargo area is impinged upon by the AMG hot-rodding treatment—the 201-hp electric motor and 5-kWh battery had to go somewhere—but there's still plenty enough room for a couple of carry-ons. (And that's while keeping the back seats available for two terrified passengers.) view from inside a car showing a yellow sports car on the highway / Car and Driver / Yellow gang! The drive from Atlanta to New Orleans is close to 500 miles and seven hours, if you do it without meandering. But we plan to meander, so we leave early on Friday and resolve to get there when we get there. We begin our trek on the highway, rolling up on another yellow fellow, a C7 Corvette Z06 whose driver pays absolutely no attention to the vibrant AMG. I hate that. It's not like I want to race, but a little blip of the throttle, maybe? A little acknowledgment that the four-door in the next lane hits 60 mph in 2.4 seconds? I don't expect genuflecting from a Corvette driver, but a little game-recognize-game is part of the fun of road trips, even if the only things your cars have in common are the color and stupid amounts of horsepower. yellow highperformance car parked near a bear statue outside a store / Ezra Dyer / Car and Driver Our first stop is a Buc-ee's, and it happens soon thanks to the GT63's 20-mpg EPA highway rating (and maybe, to some extent, my right foot). I'm delighted to discover that the AMG's gas cap is emblazoned with the word hybrid, as if that means you'll be laying eyes on it infrequently. We avail ourselves of the Buc-ee's bathrooms but not of its brisket sandwiches. It's still early for that. Image no longer available What's the tow rating on a fifth-gen Explorer again? Pushing into Alabama, I begin noticing a phenomenon that I dub the Alabama Road Train. I'm not sure the practice is, how do you say, legal, but it consists of one roadworthy car towing two others that are in various states of decrepitude. The first one we see is a Ford Explorer towing another Explorer, with what looks like a Honda Civic hitched to that. I say looks like because the rear of the car is annihilated such that it's hard to tell what it is, other than a sedan that met a tree or phone pole backwards and at high speed. I'm fascinated by this whole tableau. Is it hard to steer this thing down the road? And what's up with that poor Explorer up front? Its tow rating is 5000 pounds at best, and it's hauling maybe 8000 pounds of junk. There are all sorts of problems at play here, so I hammer down to put it all in the rearview mirror and aim the AMG for my favorite spot in the Cotton State: the Alabamabahn. view of a road and landscape from a vehicles perspective / Car and Driver / Somewhere in Alabama. Heather is napping when we reach the Alabamabahn, and that's for the best, because this is one place where I can hypothetically exploit the GT63's ridiculous abilities. I can't tell you where this place is, and if I could I still wouldn't, but I'll describe it in general terms: the highway splits, with northbound and southbound lanes on concrete pilings over wetlands. They're not connected, so a cop going the opposite direction can't cut over and get behind you. It's also very straight, so if the coast looks clear it really is—there are no on-ramps or overpasses, nowhere to hide. It's the sort of place where, with little to no traffic, one can have some good fun with 831 horsepower (hypothetically, for the sake of argument). Maybe you know where I'm talking about. I've said enough. car parked in front of a colorful mural / Ezra Dyer / Car and Driver / The gator's gonna git'cha! In Mississippi, we divert off the highway to drive along the Gulf Coast. On our way to the Bayou restaurant in Ocean Springs, we're at a red light when some kids in an old SLK with the top down spot the AMG and hold their phones up over the windshield header to take photos. The kids are all right. a bright yellow sports car parked on the side of the road / Car and Driver / Heather, mostly unaware of how quickly we got through Alabama. Making our way into Louisiana, I decide to take a random exit and discover a bizarre scene. The exit maybe went somewhere once upon a time, but it now dead-ends in a swamp. And evidently, the ghost exit is now a dumping ground—mostly for old tires, but also furniture and mattresses. It's gross, but I also feel like I've driven onto the set of a post-apocalyptic movie, the Benz's vibrant yellow perfection standing out among the bleak gray swamp and mounds of black tires. The lone stop sign is haphazardly nailed to a phone pole that leans at a 45-degree angle. Even a set designer would look at that and say, No, too disaster-cliché. I hate that this is here, but I'm glad I took a random detour and saw it, because that's what road trips are about. That evening we roll into New Orleans and discover that the Super Bowl atmosphere is something to behold even if you never set foot in the stadium. Everybody knows they're at the place that everyone's watching, and there's a giddiness in the air. On Sunday, Bourbon Street is shut down and Fox's pregame broadcast is happening right in the middle of the street—we walk behind Terry Bradshaw, Gronk, and company as they're on air. This year's game is in Santa Clara, outside San Francisco, but the pregame festivities are happening downtown, and San Francisco's fun even when it's not hosting the Super Bowl. fox super bowl broadcast on bourbon street / Ezra Dyer / Car and Driver / Yes, you can pretty much walk right into the broadcast at the public Super Bowl Fan Experience. Broadcasting set for a sports event with commentators seated at a table outdoors / Car and Driver / This year, this will be happening in downtown San Francisco. Get in the car! I'm not sure Patriots fans want to road-trip to California (save for the West Coast expats Pats crowd), but Seattle to San Francisco's only about 800 miles. Chickens fans, start your engines. I promise it'll be worth the trip. Headshot of Ezra Dyer Senior Editor Ezra Dyer is a Car and Driver senior editor and columnist. He's now based in North Carolina but still remembers how to turn right. He owns a 2009 GEM e4 and once drove 206 mph. Those facts are mutually exclusive.

General Motors won't remove Allison Transmission badging from its heavy-duty pickup trucks after all. The announcement follows news from last fall, when GM planned to stop using Allison badging moving forward and would hunt down and remove existing badges. There's been no actual change to the transmission hardware, but GM and Allison's messy breakup has been smoothed over for now. The longstanding partnership between General Motors and Allison Transmission hit a rough patch last year, when it was announced that GM's heavy-duty trucks would lose their Allison badges. GM's decision to not only stop using Allison branding on new trucks, but to hunt down any unsold models and remove Allison badging from them, was proof of just how vitriolic the breakup was. 2024 chevrolet silverado 2500hd zr2 bison / 2024 Chevrolet SIlverado 2500HD ZR2 Bison Despite the hiccup, it seems GM and Allison have smoothed over their differences, with the two brands renewing their partnership. A spokesperson for General Motors confirmed to Car and Driver that Allison badging will be added back to the production line for the 2026 Chevy Silverado HD and 2026 GMC Sierra HD lineups. In spite of the drama between the two brands, the hardware in the pickups was never affected. Since 2020, GM has been using the 10L 1000 10-speed automatic transmission, which it co-developed with Ford. The new 10-speed was ultimately verified and signed off on by Allison, though the transmission company doesn't actually build the units that wind up in trucks. GM hasn't said exactly how many HD trucks were produced without the Allison branding, but as far as we know, there are no plans to retrofit them with the badge. Relationships can be tough, and sometimes you need to buy flowers, which is exactly what we imagine someone on the Allison team did in this case. ➡️ Skip the lot. Let Car and Driver help you find your next car. Headshot of Jack Fitzgerald Associate News Editor Jack Fitzgerald's love for cars stems from his as yet unshakable addiction to Formula 1. After a brief stint as a detailer for a local dealership group in college, he knew he needed a more permanent way to drive all the new cars he couldn't afford and decided to pursue a career in auto writing. By hounding his college professors at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee, he was able to travel Wisconsin seeking out stories in the auto world before landing his dream job at Car and Driver. His new goal is to delay the inevitable demise of his 2010 Volkswagen Golf.

This unusual supercar is a 1991 Isdera Imperator 108i Series 2, and it's coming up for auction on February 27 in Miami via RM Sotheby's. Isdera built just 30 Imperators over a nine-year span, and all of them pack a Mercedes-Benz V-8 behind the passenger compartment. The Imperator made around 300 horsepower, and Isdera claimed it could go from zero to 60 mph in 5.1 seconds and hit a top speed of 175 mph. Germany has produced some of the world's most iconic supercars, from the Porsche Carrera GT and the Mercedes-Benz SLS AMG to the BMW M1 and the Audi R8. With so much automotive passion brewing within its borders, Germany is also home to plenty of lesser-known, albeit equally awesome, machines. Now, one of the most eye-catching creations from Deutschland, the 1991 Isdera Imperator 108i Series 2, is coming up for auction via RM Sotheby's later this month. It pairs wild styling with serious performance chops and an intriguing history. For Sale Near You See all results for used cars for sale near 98661 1991 isdera imperator 108 series 2 / 1991 Isdera Imperator 108i Series 2 / RM Sotheby's Isdera was founded in 1982 by a German designer named Eberhard Schulz. Initially employed at Porsche, Schulz then moved to a design firm called B+B, where he crafted the Mercedes-Benz CW311. The concept car was created as a spiritual successor to the iconic 300SL and was originally built without approval from Mercedes before the German giant granted permission to use the iconic three-pointed star logo. Schulz wanted to put the CW311 into production, so he started his own company and launched the Imperator 108i. While the Imperator no longer wore a Mercedes badge, its sleek wedge shape was clearly an evolution of the CW311, and the car packed a Mercedes-Benz V-8 mounted amidships. 1991 isdera imperator 108i series 2 / 1991 Isdera Imperator 108i Series 2 / RM Sotheby's 1991 isdera imperator 108i series 2 / 1991 Isdera Imperator 108i Series 2 Under the dramatic skin, the Imperator was motivated by a Mercedes-Benz M119 32-valve, 5.0-liter V-8 engine mated to a five-speed manual gearbox. Output was around 300 horsepower, and Isdera claimed a zero-to-60-mph time of 5.1 seconds and a top speed of 175 mph, impressive numbers for the era. This example was sold new to someone in Japan, although it remained left-hand drive and comes with copies of its original order form and correspondence. There are fewer than 1400 miles on the odometer, and the Imperator is expected to fetch between $650,000 and $850,000 when it comes up for auction in Miami on February 27. ➡️ Skip the lot.
